skill.name: pydeseq
skill.description: Differential gene expression analysis for bulk RNA-seq count matrices using a DESeq2-like workflow in Python; use when you need Wald tests, FDR correction, and optional LFC shrinkage for condition/batch/covariate designs.
skill.summary: Run DESeq2-style differential gene expression analysis on bulk RNA-seq count matrices using Python
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 177 HIGH-severity 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives caused by the static analyzer miscategorizing bioinformatics statistical terms (padj, dispersion, Wald test statistics) as cryptographic operations. All 153 MEDIUM-severity 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are false positives from Markdown code formatting backticks in documentation files. Three filesystem writes and two network URLs are legitimate operations: the script saves analysis results to a user-specified output directory, and the URL references the project source repository. No malicious intent, no data exfiltration, and no command injection vectors were found. This is a legitimate bioinformatics analysis skill safe to publish.

low_findings.title: File Write Operations for Results Output
low_findings.description: Three filesystem write operations detected. Two are in Markdown documentation showing example pickle save patterns. One is in the analysis script (run_deseq2_analysis.py:181) which saves analysis results to a user-specified output directory. These are legitimate operations for a data analysis skill that produces output files.

low_findings.title: Source Repository URL Reference
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL to the project source repository on GitHub. This is a legitimate citation in the skill documentation for attribution purposes. No data exfiltration or unauthorized network behavior detected.

content.user_title: Analyze Differential Gene Expression with PyDESeq2
content.value_statement: Bulk RNA-seq data is difficult to analyze without specialized statistical tools. This skill enables differential gene expression analysis using a DESeq2-like workflow in Python, with Wald tests, FDR correction, and LFC shrinkage.
actual_capabilities: Load and validate RNA-seq count matrices and sample metadata from CSV files
Run DESeq2 normalization including size factor estimation and dispersion fitting
Perform Wald tests for differential expression with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction
Apply LFC shrinkage for stable visualization and gene ranking
Save analysis results to CSV and generate volcano and MA plots
Support single-factor, multi-factor, and interaction design formulas
limitations: Not a replacement for the original R DESeq2 package; results may differ slightly
Cannot analyze single-cell RNA-seq data; designed for bulk RNA-seq only
Requires non-negative integer count matrices; pre-processed or normalized data is not valid input
Large datasets may require significant memory; consider filtering low-count genes first

use_cases.title: Identify Differentially Expressed Genes in Treatment vs Control
use_cases.description: Run a complete differential expression analysis comparing treated and control samples from bulk RNA-seq count data, including normalization, Wald tests, and multiple testing correction.
use_cases.target_user: Biomedical researchers with bulk RNA-seq count matrices

use_cases.title: Integrate Differential Expression into Python Pipelines
use_cases.description: Incorporate DESeq2-style analysis into existing Python workflows using pandas DataFrames and AnnData objects for downstream analysis, QC, and reporting.

use_cases.title: Migrate from R DESeq2 to Python
use_cases.description: Convert existing R DESeq2 workflows to Python while maintaining comparable statistical methods and results for differential gene expression analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Data scientists transitioning from R to Python

prompt_templates.title: Basic Differential Expression Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run differential expression analysis on my count matrix. Load counts.csv and metadata.csv, filter low-count genes, run the DESeq2 pipeline, and identify significant genes with padj less than 0.05.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-factor Design with Batch Correction
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform differential expression analysis adjusting for batch effects. Use the design formula batch plus condition and the contrast condition treated versus control. Save results to CSV files.

prompt_templates.title: Full Workflow with Visualization
prompt_templates.prompt: Load count data from counts.csv and metadata from metadata.csv. Use design formula condition. Run DESeq2, apply LFC shrinkage, generate a volcano plot, and save all results to the output directory.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complete analysis with LFC shrinkage and plotting

prompt_templates.title: Multiple Pairwise Group Comparisons
prompt_templates.prompt: Run differential expression across multiple treatment groups. For each treatment group, compare against the control group and save separate results files. Report the number of significant genes for each comparison.

output_examples.input: Run differential expression analysis on treated versus control RNA-seq data
output_examples.output: Loaded count matrix with 6 samples by 20000 genes. After filtering, retained 18000 genes. DESeq2 fitting complete. Found 1243 significant genes with padj less than 0.05. Results saved to deseq2_results.csv.

output_examples.input: Generate a volcano plot from the analysis results
output_examples.output: Volcano plot generated and saved to volcano_plot.png. The plot shows log2 fold change on the x-axis and negative log10 adjusted p-value on the y-axis. Significant genes are highlighted in red.

output_examples.input: Run multi-factor analysis with batch correction
output_examples.output: Design formula batch plus condition. DESeq2 fitting complete after adjusting for 2 batch groups. Found 856 significant genes with padj less than 0.05. Results saved with both unshrunken and shrunken LFC estimates.

best_practices: Always verify count matrix orientation is samples by genes before running analysis
Filter low-count genes with total counts less than 10 to improve statistical power
Include batch variables in the design formula when samples come from different sequencing runs
anti_patterns: Using normalized or transformed counts as input; DESeq2 expects raw integer counts
Running multiple statistical tests on the same dataset without correcting for multiple comparisons
Interpreting shrunken log fold changes for significance testing; use unshrunken p-values instead

faq.question: What is the minimum number of samples needed for differential expression analysis?
faq.answer: PyDESeq2 requires at least 2 samples per condition for statistical testing, but 3 or more per group is recommended for reliable dispersion estimates.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for single-cell RNA-seq data?
faq.answer: No. This skill implements a DESeq2-like workflow designed for bulk RNA-seq data. Single-cell data requires different normalization and statistical approaches.

faq.question: How do I specify a multi-factor design?
faq.answer: Use Wilkinson notation like batch plus condition. Put adjustment variables first and the variable of interest last in the formula.

faq.question: What is the difference between pvalue and padj in the results?
faq.answer: pvalue is the raw Wald test p-value. padj is the p-value adjusted for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg FDR method.

faq.question: Should I use LFC shrinkage for all analyses?
faq.answer: LFC shrinkage is recommended for visualization and gene ranking but not for significance testing. Use unshrunken p-values for determining significance.

faq.question: How should I handle missing values in metadata?
faq.answer: Remove samples with missing metadata values before running the analysis. DESeq2 cannot handle missing values in the design formula variables.
